author: @gringene_bio
text:
From my Carpentries training, I learnt about one way of breaking learning down into four categories: Novices (who have unconscious ignorance; they don't know what they don't know), Learners (who have conscious ignorance; they know what they don't know), Masters (who have conscious knowledge; they know what they know), and Experts (who have unconscious knowledge; they don't know what they know). Novices make lots of mistakes, and don't know why. Experts almost always do the right thing, and don't know why.

Experts are often bad teachers (they've forgotten what they know), and Novices are often bad students (they need a proper foundation to learn).

author: @salaciouscreations4323
text: Well thanks to you.    I bought an all metal electric fire and gutted it to leave a frame. Added 20kg of tincan sand batterys with copper pipe cores and used those  large money boxes for the core burner for methanol.     It holds 1ltr of methanol and burns for 2 hours and the sand batteries keep it warm for another 2 hours.    My total winter bill was £300 for heating.     I looked at your veg oil candles   and applied what i learnt building my methanol heater.   Tin can on the bottom for the fuel and holes atound the top for air flow    2nd can chimney with 2 holes in the bottom as that feeds the tip of the flame more air and 2 holes so the heat doesnt kill the flame.  3rd can is a sand battery with a copper core

replies.author: @salaciouscreations4323
replies.text: I been testing them for the past week and just used the first 1ltr bottle of veg oil.    They typically burn for 12 hours until the wick stops wicking.       So this yeah we have only bought £100 of methanol  and will use the veg oil burners as the all day on heat with the methanol to bring up the room temps.      Given if you shop around you can get 5ltr of oil for £6.    It should refine the heating winter cost down to hopefull £150

replies.author: @salaciouscreations4323
replies.text: I believe in sand batteries on making more of waste heat.    It was a long road last year getting methanol to work for us  but the lost cost was the learning experience.        I have smoke detectors and monoxide detectors in the rooms where all this stuff is used and it is a very viable option to be warm and comfortable all winter.

author: @kensmith5694
text:
I am going to disagree slightly.  Someone who is wrong can be respected the first 10 times but by the 11th time of having it explained to them that they are wrong about something it is no longer reasonable to demand they be respected.  Perhaps being sorry for them is what should happen then.  You will encounter the sort I mean in politics more than anywhere else.  They will make a claim about some matter of politics and someone will prove them wrong (perhaps by pointing them to a bit of history) and then the next day they will make the same claim again and so on.
I have been around a long time and have a very good memory.  This means I can remember a very large number of mistakes I have made.  I will tend to warn people of the errors when I see them about to repeat one of them.

author: @crypticnomad
text: I've never really been concerned with becoming an expert in really anything. That takes too much time and usually competence is good enough for most things I need. It takes a fraction of the time to become competent than it does to become a master. For me it seems much more useful to start off with the basic idea that I'm often wrong, am probably wrong now, have mastered nothing, but I am competent enough to prove myself wrong. I only say "I might be right" after I put a bunch of real effort into proving myself wrong and bounce the idea off of others to see if they can prove me wrong. I'm a contrarian by nature and basically assume the masses are wrong. Then I work to prove myself wrong on that assumption. Due to that basic tendency I think in terms of first or core principles. The masters of basically anything from martial arts, to electronics, to whatever have spent the most time working on the basics of whatever field. They are masters of the basics with some random knowledge from lots of experience sprinkled in for flavor.

text: I've no idea if I'm an expert or just exercising expertise. I've been an arborist (retired now) for decades, but never took the academic side to degree level. I did end up as an arb officer doing inspections and fault/disease diagnosis for contracting out work, but when I decided to 'hang my harness up', I helped with the practical field assessment of my potential replacements. Bizarrely they were all more academically qualified than I, yet were shite at species identification or observational skills. The dept had to take on two to replace me. That was years ago yet education continues to be dumbed down.

replies.author: @kensmith5694
replies.text:
I have hired an "arborist" (note the quotes) to look after the big tree in my yard.  This was years ago.  They had the bit of paper and all.  Still when it came down to it, they and their crew made a huge mess of the job.  The dead branches were still all up there.  Clearly I was dealing with an "expert" in your field.  I have seen the same thing go on in my field.[1] It saddens me greatly to see it.

[1] Mostly I do electronics.  The case in question was someone hired to design something.  Among the design requirements was that the outer metal housing must be isolated from the electronics inside well enough to withstand 35V.  The hired engineer insisted on connection the chassis directly to circuit ground.  I saw that and suggested the people hiring him go find a new guy.

author: @Vibe77Guy
text:
Unfortunately I keep butting heads over the viral maths problems of 8÷2(2+2)=X and 6÷2(1+2)=X
The "experts" claim that these are not X=1.
Yet 120"÷2π(11"+1")=1.59 revolutions.  Because any answer in square inches makes no sense.

replies.author: @PurpleAlienPlanet
replies.text:
The issue you're seeing with these viral math problems is that they're examples of the order of operations as they stand today. If you go back 100 years, the ÷ symbol used to indicate that everything that followed was in the denominator. This is not the current meaning. Instead, if you take 8 ÷ 2(2+2) you need to solve the brackets first, leading to: 8 ÷ 2 x 4. now the order of operations becomes important: both the division and multiplication has the same order, in which case the rule says the expression is evaluated from left to right, meaning first divide 8 by 2, then multiply the result with 4, leading to 16.
If you want to have the expression after the ÷ sign be fully under in the denominator, use brackets: 8÷(2(2+2)). Having said that, I hate those viral math problems because the way they are written is deliberately confusing even if technically correct - and that's the reason they're everywhere. Adding brackets to the original equation would take the confusion away: (8÷2)x(2+2), but that's not going to go viral anywhere...

replies.author: @Vibe77Guy
replies.text:
@PurpleAlienPlanet  
For over 20000 years the development of Mathematics strove to provide a language to describe the physical laws of nature. Such Mathematics is only useful when it can be used to do that job. It eventually became mature enough to actually predict the outcomes of certain combinations of factors.
Then the invention of electronic calculators was born, and programmers took shortcuts and have been doing serious damage to that ability. 
Nothing can promote a lie like complex statistics. 
The most basic evidence of this is the continued viral videos that claim 8÷2(2+2)≠1 and 6÷2(1+2)≠1
All because some middle school teachers, in an an oversimplified attempt to get the basics across, came up with PEMDAS or BOMDAS. And then computer and calculator programmers ran wirh it. Those methods are great, in as far as they go, but they are still oversimplifications.

To err is human, to really foul things up, use a computer. GIGO! Garbage in=garbage out. Are basic programming warnings for a reason.

When you put units of measurement in the equation, the incorrect viral methodology falls apart. 
Distance ÷ circumference = revolutions 
D÷2πr= revolutions, not square inches.

replies.author: @kensmith5694
replies.text:
@undernetjack Your claims are out dated. 
 I suggest you check the recent numbers.  More recent studies on the subject have shown that there is more than enough to make PV a source of power meeting the protected need in the future. ei: Larger than today.
Air conditioning and refrigeration don't need rare materials and can be done very efficiently.  In North America, most houses that change from a furnace to a heat pump, including air conditioning, see a net reduction in energy use for heating and cooling.
There is even a system that will soon become common that doesn't need any working gas other than nitrogen.  Look up thermoacoustic heat pumps.  It turns out that new versions of these are going to cost less to produce than the current designs.
